sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Seven — functions. This is one of the bigger ideas in math, and it shows up in every course you take after this. The good news — once you see it, you can't unsee it. It's everywhere.

Think about a vending machine. You punch in code A four. Out comes a Snickers. Punch in B two — you get Doritos. Same code always gives the same snack. That right there — that's a function. A rule that takes one input and gives back exactly one output. Same input always gives the same output. That's the whole concept. Everything else in this module is just notation and edge cases around that one idea.

Six things to cover. One — what a function actually is, formally. Two — function notation, that f-of-x thing you've seen. Three — domain and range, the inputs and outputs. Four — how to evaluate a function. Five — the vertical line test, for spotting functions on graphs. And six — real-world functions, where this all shows up outside the textbook.

Formal definition. A function is a rule where every input has exactly one output. Notice the words — every input has ONE output. Not zero, not two, exactly one. If an input could give you two different outputs, it's not a function. That's the line. We'll come back to it when we talk about the vertical line test.

Function notation. We write functions as f of x — pronounced 'f of x', with parentheses around the x. The f is the name of the function — it can be g, h, anything. The x is the input. Whatever's on the other side of the equals sign is the rule. So f of x equals two x plus three means: take an input, multiply by two, add three. That's the function.

Evaluating a function means plugging in for x. Same as expressions in Module One, just with the f-of-x notation. Example. f of x equals two x plus three. What's f of five? Replace x with five. Two times five, plus three. Ten plus three. Thirteen. So f of five equals thirteen. We say 'f at five' or 'f of five' — both fine.

Quick warning. f of two does NOT mean f times two. It looks like multiplication but it isn't. f of two means evaluate the function f at the input two. If you forget this, you'll fail the next quiz, so commit it to memory. f of x — the x is inside the function, not multiplied by it.

Domain and range. Domain is the set of all inputs you're allowed to put in. Range is the set of all outputs the function can produce. For f of x equals two x plus three, the domain is all real numbers — you can plug in anything. The range is also all real numbers — you can get any output. But for some functions, like one over x, you can't put in zero — division by zero is undefined. So that function's domain excludes zero.

The vertical line test is the visual shortcut for spotting functions on graphs. Take any vertical line. Move it across the graph. If it ever crosses the curve at more than one point, the relation is NOT a function — because that would mean one x value mapping to multiple y values. If every vertical line hits the graph at most once, you've got a function. Try it on a circle — fails. On a parabola opening up — passes.
Quick check. Given f of x equals three x minus four, find f of two and f of negative one. Pause and try.

Time. f of two — three times two, minus four. Six minus four. Two. f of negative one — three times negative one, minus four. Negative three minus four. Negative seven. So f of two is two, f of negative one is negative seven. If you got both, you've got the mechanic down.

Real-world functions are everywhere. Cost of buying x apples at fifty cents each — c of x equals zero point five times x. That's a function. Convert Celsius to Fahrenheit — f of c equals nine fifths c plus thirty-two. That's a function. Tax on income x at fifteen percent — t of x equals zero point one five x. Function. Anytime there's a rule that turns one number into one other number, you can write it as a function.
One more. The function g of x equals x squared. Find g of three and g of negative three. Pause and solve. Bonus question — is the result the same? Why?

Welcome back. g of three is three squared, which is nine. g of negative three is negative three squared — also nine. Same output! That's allowed in a function. The rule says one input gives ONE output, not that different inputs must give different outputs. Squaring just happens to send positive and negative versions to the same place. Totally fine.

Recap. A function takes one input, gives exactly one output. Notation is f of x — and that's NOT multiplication. Domain is allowed inputs, range is possible outputs. Evaluate by substituting. Vertical line test on a graph — crosses more than once, not a function. And functions are everywhere outside the textbook — cost, conversion, tax, time.

Important reminder — this video is the overview. To actually master functions, here's the path. One — read OpenStax Chapter three point five on relations and functions. Two — work through the Khan Academy practice set on evaluating functions. Three — do the assignment in your dashboard: classify five relations, evaluate ten function inputs across three functions, and state domain and range. Four — book a check-in with your advisor, especially if function notation is still feeling weird. This video is maybe fifteen percent of learning this module. Practice does the rest. See you in Module Eight, where we put functions on a graph.
